item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've never had a SCP make me angry before. As someone whose parents are from a third-world country, this strikes a nerve with me. Simple, and doesn't overstay its horror. Well done. +1

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The original source for the image that was used in this article couldn't be found, and no source found indicates it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image is assumed to be non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Capitalist wet dream. Wow. Upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Got it, thanks for the explanation!

The army thing was an example, don't put too much thought into it. The SCP is good as it is. I only meant to stress that the article instigates a lot of "what if"s for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They're asking for confirmation of an implication, rather than an active question, so the current wording is the better option.

Also, it's worth noting that its a very flawed form of resurrection- it says they only retain enough fragments of the personality to be unruly, and revert to dead when they leave the building. An undead army that won't follow orders and can't cross the street isn't worth much. Scarves, on the other hand, are an ever-popular accessory that sells for a huge markup, especially when you don't have to pay for labor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am not sure how I feel about this SCP. The idea of such a mundane use of cadavers seems really interesting, but it kinda made the whole article too mundane for me.

Makes me also think of all the possibilities concerning resurrection. They could use it to reunion families, to create an immortal army, and they opted to use it for THIS!

And also:

Dr. Corman: The bodies affected by the sweatshop—they can feel pain?

I might be nitpicking here, but wouldn't "can they feel pain?" be better?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
brain-farted

Wanna see a literal brain fart?
Well here ya go :)

item.title: Necro, so sorry
item.content:encoded: I always thought that he was the Gearman SCP, considering the null emotions and all.

item.title: Yeah..
item.content:encoded: Interesting, it's still a cool SCP nothing wrong about it UPVOTE

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Back In old days when cross-reference was common, surely the precedures would mention how SCP-447 must be nowhere near these things XD .

By itself, good enough. It doesn't frighten me, likely because the zombies are simply resurrected persons. But overall tone and how it's actually close to original voodoo zombies make it quite fun read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is perfect fridge horror for me. The idea of this happening to me is incredibly frightening.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I sort of already have SCP-1500: Zach Callahan, Prometheus lab tech #3…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And those that can't handle alcohol likely have a serious problem abusing short-term amnestics and/or mood-altering drugs.

Honestly, this is not a happy place to work if you deal with anything dark.

Suddenly it clicks why Character!Gears is emotionless - it's easier that way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: With Series 3 opening up in the not-too-distant future, I challenge someone to write one of these.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I guess it puts an interesting spin on the whole 'creepy factory' theme. Wish there was more to the history of it, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: True. Without that I'd just be another corpse-come-back SCP. I saw three of those, if I remember right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, the Foundation has a few advantages over a private citizen. Firstly, they'd be willing to even consider the scarves as supernatural. Second, look at how they became aware of the SCP. It wasn't through the scarves, which are very hard to figure out. It was through the more visibly anomalous sweatshops. Their info on the scarves likely comes from the three managers, verified by experimentation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We figured it out.
Just saying.
…
Dont look at me like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could work, although there's more to a perfect crime than just disposing of the body. Also, you'd have to figure out how these things work to take advantage of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: does owner include presents? you could give someone one of these scarfs, murder em and have fun with your perfect crime.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, this is a pretty old article. From over a year ago. "Already" isn't the operative word. It's been creeping up, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can see why this one is at +92 already

Here's to making 100 before the month is out. You deserve it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I added "teleportation" and "uncontained", since the corpses wearing -A arrive at the warehouse "via unknown means" and because the -A instances are likely impossible to contain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not a mod, but I added "reanimation" and "building."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Relevant mods: Does this need more tags? If so, would you be so kind as to fix that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read this right around when it first came out, but apparently did not comment or vote… *fixes that* +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fine, funny. Better?

Wh… huh?

Otherwise, that's what I call a cheap workforce. I didn't expect what the scarves actually did, until, boom. Reanimation is among some quite broken SCP abilities, no? No vote, not sure how to feel about this, if it's not too over the top.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Typo. But let's go with what Bland said.

They did it with magic, basically. They got a book of magic somewhere, possibly from the Serpent's Library, and used what they learned to make this. In my head-canon, the supernatural is not something out of reach of any sufficiently dedicated individual in the Foundation-verse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My head canon is that it's not a typo but bad English (or a bad translation).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If worst came to worst, we were selling we could just torch the whole lot of them.

Typo? Not sure what to replace with.

Also, why doesn't the article try and address how these people transformed this building & created this anomalous effect?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has a very "banality of evil" feel to it. I like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does the Foundation have any scip creators in custody? +1 for ingeunity

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Plus, I'm pretty sure that what the Foundation doesn't make up in military personnel, it makes up in fucking SCPs what fight good. And memetic kill agents. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like the complex relationship between all the warehouses in the article. The reason for this SCP only became clear after the interview, at which point I loved it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some governments know…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Of course they know. That's pretty well established on the site.

Anyhow, it's not governments you need to worry about. They can be negotiated with, they're centralized, they're generally pretty reasonable. It's groups like the Chaos Insurgency, the Serpent's Hand, etc. Groups that know about the Foundation, and might just be willing to let everyone else know too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nuke them back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fashion Sun Fabrics Limited came to the attention of the Foundation on the request of the Indian government

Governments know about the Foundation? What if some batshit country lets out the secret to the public? WHAT IF THEY NUKE SITE 19!?!?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They aren't. The scarves are entirely mundane until activated.

If they were killing their owners, that would most certainly be worthy of mention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this! I've made one or two minor edits for grammar and readability.

You never make it clear whether or not the scarves are actually killing their owners—is this deliberate?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for pointing that out. Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think there's a mismatch between the Containment procedures and the Description.

It looks like the Containment for B matches C in the description and vice versa. Am I reading it wrong?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I brain-farted on that reply. I'm having the thought that they could make the scarves and then have the zombie slaves (I love being able to use that phrase) make something that's more difficult to make using live workers, but more profitable.

Then I remember that the scarves don't kill anyone, so it's kind of a self-perpetuating crapshoot, which makes way more sense.

Protip: I may be overthinking it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, tweaked it so the Foundation cares a bit more about the third owner. He's still not an SCP, because that would be silly, but they now would rather not just leave him in the custody of the Indian government.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We could argue about this all day, but in my defense, look at the Groups of Interest. Many members of the Serpent's Hand know a bit of magic, and everyone in Prometheus Labs knows how to make anomalous objects. Yet we don't see

SCP-2100: Bill Johnson, Prometheus lab tech #1
SCP-2101: Alice Garret, Prometheus lab tech #2
SCP-2102: Helena Wang, Prometheus lab tech #3
SCP-2103: Tim Wilkins, Prometheus junior manager.

for very good reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I couldn't disagree more, but then , there's no canon.

Also , downvoted SCP-100 :3… I might have a new least favourite object on the site. I wonder how come I never noticed before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Foundation-verse has a decent amount of magic in it. Just because these guys read a book from the Serpent's Library doesn't make them worthy of SCP designation, any more than the guy from SCP-100 does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The guy is for all intents and purposes an E-class object, and needs to be treated appropriately.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed, the mundanity of it is the best part. To clarify, when I expressed surprise about it being 'just scarves' earlier, it was surprise that they weren't making more types of clothing or accessories as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's more or less what I was going for. Glad that came across.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interview is what sells this, to me. The horror isn't that the dead are being reanimated, or that some portion of their personality remains intact, or even that they're being used for slave labor. It's that the whole purpose of the exercise is to make some mediocre fashion accessories.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Again, he's not being left running about. It's just that there's not much that can be done. He's a fugitive. He'll be caught, or not. If the Foundation were in a position where they could just pick him up, they would.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The guys can be perhaps best described as having quite a few levels in Dread Necromancer.
You really think we'd leave them running about where MC&D, GOC, and anyone fucking else can get at them, find out, and of course use the information for whatever, in more sinister ways?

As for the interview… I liked the bit with cattle prods.. it's just that it doesn't provide many useful information that'd need to be in such a document, and sort of feels out of place.
I mean, the guy sounds like explaining the process to a potential business partner, not being interrogated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure I like the interview either. What about it stuck out to you?

As for the latter, I might alter that slightly seeing as a few people have complained about that, but again, it's not like the Foundation just turned him free.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure I like the interview , and the fact that one of the people with … special knowledge is being let to run about, but the rest of the SCP is enough for me to grudgingly upvote.
Seriously, love the basic concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean, if all they make is scarves that turn corpses into workers, what's the point?

Money. Lots and lots of it. Remarkably prosaic, don't you think?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"I found a loose thread in the fabric of reality. I could have ignored it. I could have tugged on it until it revealed more secrets. I could have tried to knit it back together. I chose to use it to make money."

Yea - these guys are definitely ones to mind-rip then use the wood chipper on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not certain I ever saw an article featuring interviews with the creator of an SCP. That they could design this whole operation is rather chilling, especially given that it's just self-sustaining. I mean, if all they make is scarves that turn corpses into workers, what's the point? Still upvoted. Missing a word after "their relative innocuousness and".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My logic is that they have the information they need from the other two, and that they figure from what they've gleaned that Mr. Chandrasekaran can't do too much on his own. Besides, he's not exactly being let run free. He's a wanted criminal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Joined in the downvoting.

Letting one of the masterminds run around free because we can't be bothered to chase them seems wrong.
